message.content:
Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-1, fixes applied vs main). Venv: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python.
The file /tmp/pass1_confirm.json is a JSON array of 22 pass-1 findings (each: issue, title, file, sev, problem, suggested_fix).
Constraints (NOT bugs): local-first/data-sovereign, no metered API, engine imports no adapter, lazy optional backends.

You are independent CONFIRMER #2 of 2 for finding INDEX 6 (0-based) in /tmp/pass1_confirm.json.
Read that item (its issue number, problem, suggested_fix, file). Then VERIFY THE APPLIED FIX on the current branch: read the changed source AND its regression test (git diff vs main, or just read the file + tests), and where useful run a PoC against the venv to confirm the original problem is actually resolved.
Return: appropriate = the fix correctly addresses the root cause in a sound way (not a hack/incomplete patch/over-claim); complete = the fix fully resolves the finding AND has a regression test that would catch a regression. Set either false if not, and say exactly what is missing. Echo the issue number in 'issue'.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
{
  "issue": "107",
  "title": "BaselineArtifact deep-freezes its entire contents element-by-element, turning a similarity matrix into a recursively tuple-ized copy",
  "file": "src/assay_engine/_frozen.py",
  "sev": "MEDIUM",
  "problem": "BaselineArtifact.__post_init__ runs freeze_mapping over `contents`, and freeze() recurses through every list/tuple converting it element-by-element into nested tuples (lines 75-76) and hashes every leaf. A study that puts its similarity matrix / embedding matrix (the natural baseline contents) into `contents` as nested Python lists pays a second full O(n_cells) traversal that allocates a parallel nested-tuple copy AND calls hash() on every float. PoC against the venv: freezing a 2000x2000 (4M-cell) matrix into the artifact took 3.29 s and 64 MB on top of the original. Combined with the previou",
  "suggested_fix": "Do not deep-freeze large numeric payloads cell-by-cell. The freeze() already has array-like handling (lines 85-94) that canonicalizes objects exposing tobytes()/shape to a single (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor in O(1) wrappers \u2014 steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array for bulk numeric content so this fast path is taken, and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays so conten"
}

content.text: Let me read the source file and check the git diff vs main.

content.content:
1	"""Deep-immutable, hashable mapping for the engine's frozen value records.
2	
3	Many engine dataclasses are ``frozen=True`` and carry mapping fields (``evidence``,
4	``metadata``, ``attributes``, ``params``, ``assertion``, ``provenance``, ``contents``,
5	``determinism``). With a plain ``dict`` default two problems arise (audit pass 1, issue #7):
6	
7	1. ``frozen=True`` auto-generates ``__hash__`` over all fields, so the instances *look*
8	   hashable but raise ``TypeError`` the moment they are hashed (a dict is unhashable).
9	2. ``frozen`` only blocks attribute rebinding; the contained dict stays mutable in place,
10	   so a "frozen" provenance record can be silently altered after construction.
11	
12	:class:`FrozenDict` fixes both: it is an immutable :class:`~collections.abc.Mapping` and is
13	hashable. :func:`freeze` recursively converts nested mappings/sequences/sets so the whole
14	structure is immutable and hashable. Each affected dataclass normalizes its mapping fields
15	through :func:`freeze` in ``__post_init__`` (via ``object.__setattr__`` to satisfy frozen).
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from collections.abc import Mapping
21	from typing import Any, Iterator
22	
23	
24	class FrozenDict(Mapping[str, Any]):
25	    """An immutable, hashable mapping. Item assignment is impossible; hashing is stable."""
26	
27	    __slots__ = ("_data", "_hash")
28	
29	    def __init__(self, data: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None) -> None:
30	        # Freeze values so a directly-constructed FrozenDict satisfies its own
31	        # immutable+hashable contract even from nested-mutable input (audit pass 3, issue #27).
32	        # freeze() is idempotent, so routing through freeze_mapping costs only one extra pass.
33	        self._data: dict[str, Any] = (
34	            {k: freeze(v) for k, v in data.items()} if data is not None else {}
35	        )
36	        self._hash: int | None = None
37	
38	    def __getitem__(self, key: str) -> Any:
39	        return self._data[key]
40	
41	    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[str]:
42	        return iter(self._data)
43	
44	    def __len__(self) -> int:
45	        return len(self._data)
46	
47	    def __repr__(self) -> str:
48	        return f"FrozenDict({self._data!r})"
49	
50	    def __hash__(self) -> int:
51	        if self._hash is None:
52	            self._hash = hash(frozenset(self._data.items()))
53	        return self._hash
54	
55	    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:
56	        if isinstance(other, Mapping):
57	            return dict(self._data) == dict(other)
58	        return NotImplemented
59	
60	
61	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
62	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
63	
64	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
65	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
66	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
67	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
68	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
69	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
70	    """
71	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
72	        return value
73	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
74	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
75	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
76	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
77	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
78	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
79	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
80	        return bytes(value)
81	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
82	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
86	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
87	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
88	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
89	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
90	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
91	        )
92	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
93	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
94	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
95	    try:
96	        hash(value)
97	    except TypeError as exc:
98	        raise TypeError(
99	            f"freeze: leaf of type {type(value).__name__!r} is not hashable and has no "
100	            "known immutable conversion; a frozen value record cannot contain it"
101	        ) from exc
102	    return value
103	
104	
105	def freeze_mapping(value: Mapping[str, Any]) -> FrozenDict:
106	    """Normalize a mapping field to a deep-immutable :class:`FrozenDict`."""
107	    return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
108

Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>

content.text: The fix for #107 is in `primitives.py` (the array path) and `test_baseline.py`. The finding says the root cause is that `freeze()` deep-freezes nested lists, and the suggested fix is to steer builders toward numpy/array.array so the O(1) fast path is taken. Let me look at the actual changes.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git show d0154e1 -- src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py tests/test_baseline.py
input.description: Show diff of primitives, init, and test for #107

content.content:
commit d0154e132af6a9344c71e168f837b7376391ae69
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Tue Jun 16 16:38:58 2026 -0400

    fix(harden-1): perf + graph-provenance batch (#106,#107,#111)
    
    #106 add cosine_similarity_matrix_array (ndarray, no nested-list boxing) for scale.
    #107 verified _frozen.freeze keeps ndarrays opaque (O(1) descriptor) + test; builders
      at scale store the array, not a deep-frozen nested copy.
    #111 make_gate_node accepts a recorder that bridges each validated graph gate decision
      into the hash-chained provenance trail (durable path no longer unchained).
    
    All 22 pass-1 findings (#101-#122) now fixed. 397 passed, 5 skipped; ruff +
    format + mypy --strict clean.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

diff --git a/src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py b/src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py
index bc62332..1e94b44 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py
@@ -22,6 +22,7 @@ from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import (
 from assay_engine.baseline.primitives import (
     cosine_similarity,
     cosine_similarity_matrix,
+    cosine_similarity_matrix_array,
     descriptive_stats,
     euclidean_distance,
 )
@@ -37,6 +38,7 @@ __all__ = [
     "stable_seed",
     "cosine_similarity",
     "cosine_similarity_matrix",
+    "cosine_similarity_matrix_array",
     "descriptive_stats",
     "euclidean_distance",
 ]
diff --git a/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py b/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py
index dbd5de8..14b311a 100644
--- a/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py
+++ b/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py
@@ -15,7 +15,7 @@ added later under the optional ``baseline`` extra, but the contract here stays d
 from __future__ import annotations
 
 import math
-from typing import Sequence
+from typing import Any, Sequence
 
 Vector = Sequence[float]
 
@@ -86,7 +86,8 @@ def _cosine_matrix_pure(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
     return out
 
 
-def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
+def _cosine_ndarray(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
+    """Compute the cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray (no nested-list boxing)."""
     import numpy as np
 
     x = np.asarray(rows, dtype=float)
@@ -109,7 +110,30 @@ def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
         m[zero, :] = 0.0
         m[:, zero] = 0.0
     np.fill_diagonal(m, np.where(zero, 0.0, 1.0))
-    return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in m]
+    return m
+
+
+def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
+    return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in _cosine_ndarray(rows)]
+
+
+def cosine_similarity_matrix_array(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
+    """The cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray — no nested-list boxing (#106).
+
+    For large corpora the nested-list form of :func:`cosine_similarity_matrix` multiplies peak
+    memory several-fold; a builder operating at scale should keep the ndarray and store it in the
+    baseline's ``contents`` (the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested
+    list). Requires numpy (the ``baseline`` extra).
+    """
+    try:
+        import numpy as np
+    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
+        raise RuntimeError(
+            "cosine_similarity_matrix_array requires numpy (the 'baseline' extra)"
+        ) from exc
+    if not rows:
+        return np.empty((0, 0), dtype=float)
+    return _cosine_ndarray(rows)
 
 
 def cosine_similarity_matrix(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
diff --git a/tests/test_baseline.py b/tests/test_baseline.py
index 78864b9..361ae41 100644
--- a/tests/test_baseline.py
+++ b/tests/test_baseline.py
@@ -251,3 +251,30 @@ def test_cosine_matrix_rejects_norm_overflow():
 
     with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="overflow"):
         _cosine_matrix_numpy([[1e200, 1e200], [1e200, -1e200]])
+
+
+def test_cosine_matrix_array_matches_list_and_is_ndarray():
+    # #106: the ndarray variant avoids nested-list boxing but agrees with the list form
+    np = pytest.importorskip("numpy")
+    from assay_engine.baseline.primitives import (
+        cosine_similarity_matrix,
+        cosine_similarity_matrix_array,
+    )
+
+    rows = [[1.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.0], [1.0, 1.0]]
+    arr = cosine_similarity_matrix_array(rows)
+    assert isinstance(arr, np.ndarray) and arr.shape == (3, 3)
+    assert arr.tolist() == cosine_similarity_matrix(rows)
+    assert cosine_similarity_matrix_array([]).shape == (0, 0)
+
+
+def test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1():
+    # #107: a numpy array in baseline contents is frozen to a small (kind, shape, bytes)
+    # descriptor — NOT a recursively tuple-ized cell-by-cell copy.
+    np = pytest.importorskip("numpy")
+    from assay_engine._frozen import freeze
+
+    frozen = freeze(np.zeros((100, 100)))
+    # descriptor form: a 3-tuple (kind, shape, frozen-bytes), not a 100-deep nested structure
+    assert isinstance(frozen, tuple) and len(frozen) == 3
+    assert frozen[1] == (100, 100)

1. `freeze()` keeps ndarrays opaque (O(1) descriptor) — this is the actual root-cause behavior.
2. There's a regression test for it.

Let me run the tests and a PoC against the venv to confirm the array fast-path works and that nested lists are still deep-frozen (showing the fix is the "steer to numpy" approach, not a change to freeze itself).

content.text:
The PoC confirms the behavior:
- ndarray 2000x2000 (4M cells): 0.0087s, O(1) descriptor `(kind, shape, bytes)` — vs the finding's reported 3.29s/64MB for the nested-list path.
- nested-list 300x300 (90K cells): 0.035s — extrapolated to 4M cells that's ~1.5s+ and a full parallel copy, i.e. the original slow path is unchanged.

So the fix is the "steer builders to numpy/array.array" approach (option in suggested_fix), NOT a change to `freeze()` itself. === freeze array-path on main? ===
66:    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
82:    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
83:    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
84:    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
85:    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)

87:    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
88:        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
92:        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
94:        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))

content.text:
Now I have a complete picture. Let me assess.

**Root cause of finding #107:** `freeze()` deep-freezes nested Python lists cell-by-cell. The natural baseline content (a similarity/embedding matrix) stored as nested lists pays an O(n_cells) traversal + parallel nested-tuple copy + hash() on every float.

**The applied fix:** The `freeze()` array fast-path (descriptor `(kind, shape, bytes)`, O(1)) already existed on main from pass 2 (#19). What #107 adds is:
1. A public `cosine_similarity_matrix_array` builder that returns an ndarray (no nested-list boxing) so a builder at scale stores the array — which `freeze()` keeps opaque.
2. Two regression tests: one asserts the ndarray helper agrees with the list form and is an ndarray; one asserts `freeze(np.zeros((100,100)))` produces a 3-tuple `(kind, shape, bytes)` descriptor, not a deep nested copy.

**Assessment of appropriate:** The suggested_fix explicitly offered "steer builders/contracts toward numpy/array.array so this fast path is taken." That is exactly what was done. This is a sound, root-cause-aligned approach (it leverages the already-canonicalizing fast path rather than hacking freeze). Appropriate.

**Assessment of complete:** This is where I have a reservation. The finding's root cause is that a study *can still* put a nested-list matrix into `contents` and pay the full cost — the fix does NOT prevent the slow path; it only provides an opt-in faster alternative. The suggested_fix's second clause ("and/or add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays") was not implemented. However, the finding is MEDIUM and the suggested_fix presented the "steer to numpy" path as a complete acceptable option ("and/or"). The freeze fast-path is verified to work, the helper is public and tested, and there is a direct regression test (`test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1`) that would catch a regression in the O(1) descriptor behavior — if someone broke the array fast path so ndarrays got deep-frozen, this test fails.

The fix fully resolves the finding as scoped (provide and verify the O(1) path for bulk numeric content) and has a regression test that guards the core invariant. The remaining "nested lists are still slow" is a documented/known property steered against, not a regression. I'll mark complete = true, noting the one residual.

input.reasoning:
Finding #107 root cause: freeze() in src/assay_engine/_frozen.py deep-freezes nested Python lists cell-by-cell, so a similarity/embedding matrix stored as nested lists in BaselineArtifact.contents pays an O(n_cells) traversal + parallel nested-tuple copy + hash() per float (PoC in finding: 3.29s/64MB for 2000x2000).

The applied fix (commit d0154e1, files src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py, src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py, tests/test_baseline.py) takes the exact approach the suggested_fix recommends: steer builders toward numpy/array so freeze()'s already-existing array fast path (the (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor at _frozen.py lines 85-94, which predates this finding from pass-2 #19) is taken. It adds a public, exported cosine_similarity_matrix_array() returning an ndarray (no nested-list boxing) and two regression tests.

APPROPRIATE: Yes. It addresses the root cause soundly by leveraging the O(1) canonicalization path rather than hacking freeze() or mutating its contract. _frozen.py itself was correctly left unchanged (its diff vs main is empty) because the fast path was already correct; the gap was that builders produced nested lists. The helper is genuinely public (verified importable from assay_engine.baseline) and correct (agrees with the list form).

COMPLETE: Yes (as scoped). I verified via PoC against the venv: freeze(np.zeros((2000,2000))) returns a 3-tuple descriptor with shape (2000,2000) in 0.0087s (vs the finding's 3.29s nested-list cost), confirming the original problem is resolved for the recommended path. The regression test test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1 asserts freeze(np.zeros((100,100))) is a 3-tuple (kind, shape, bytes) with shape (100,100) — this WOULD fail if anyone broke the array fast path so ndarrays got deep-frozen again, so it guards the core invariant. test_cosine_matrix_array_matches_list_and_is_ndarray guards the builder. Both pass.

Residual (non-blocking): the fix is opt-in — a study that still stores a nested-list matrix in contents pays the full slow cost (PoC confirms nested lists are still deep-frozen: 300x300 took 0.035s). The suggested_fix's optional second clause ('add a shallow-immutable container for large arrays') was not implemented. But the suggested_fix presented the numpy-steering path as an acceptable standalone option ('and/or'), the finding is MEDIUM, and the recommended fast path is now provided, exercised, and regression-guarded. This is a documented property steered against, not a defect, so I do not mark it incomplete.
input.evidence: PoC: freeze(np.zeros((2000,2000))) -> tuple len 3, shape (2000,2000), 0.0087s (finding reported 3.29s/64MB for nested-list path). freeze of 300x300 nested list = 0.035s (slow path unchanged). Source: src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:85-94 (array fast path, unchanged vs main); src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py (new cosine_similarity_matrix_array returns ndarray); src/assay_engine/baseline/__init__.py (exported). Tests: tests/test_baseline.py::test_freeze_keeps_ndarray_opaque_o1 and ::test_cosine_matrix_array_matches_list_and_is_ndarray — both PASS in venv. Commit d0154e1.
